sound_type: rising ooo-eek flight call -- sharp, rising, squealed, 1,500-5,000 Hz, ascending over 0.3-0.5 seconds. In-flight contact call. Plaintive, almost electronic. Diagnostic. E=4.
ecosystem: Wooded pond -- summer. Woodland ponds, forested swamps, flooded bottomland hardwoods, quiet backwaters. Sheltered water near mature trees with cavities. Oak-dominated stands for acorns.
season: Year-round resident in western Washington. Summer assigned for breeding/nesting focus. Cavity nesting peaks in spring; broods through summer.
family: Anatidae (fourth consecutive entry: Harlequin 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120, Wood Duck 121)

coordinates.description: Reference for wooded pond habitat in the Puget Sound lowland. Wood Ducks found in woodland ponds, riparian corridors, and forested wetlands west of the Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: Fourth consecutive Anatidae entry. Most beautiful duck in North America. Male's iridescent green/purple head with white racing stripes, chestnut breast. Rising ooo-eek flight call. Obligate cavity nester. Nest box conservation success story. Genus Aix (two species worldwide). E=4.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 4 entries (Harlequin Duck 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120, Wood Duck 121). Streak: 4 consecutive.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Aix from Greek meaning 'a kind of waterbird.' sponsa from Latin 'betrothed, promised one' -- reference to the male's ornate plumage, as if dressed for a wedding. The Wood Duck: the betrothed waterbird.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Aix -- two species: Wood Duck (Aix sponsa, North America) and Mandarin Duck (Aix galericulata, East Asia). Both extraordinarily beautiful. Both cavity nesters. Biogeographic mirror: parallel species on two continents.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes continues (fourth consecutive). The waterfowl order holds at 4. Approaching significant streak territory.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae fourth consecutive entry. Streak: 118-121. Track continuation at 122.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,500-5,000 Hz. Rising squeal. High-pitched for a duck.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2,500-4,000 Hz. The rising portion of the ooo-eek concentrates energy in the upper frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male flight call: rising ooo-eek, 1,500-5,000 Hz. Female: louder, more emphatic oo-eek oo-eek. Brood call: rapid low kuk-kuk-kuk.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call: 0.3-0.5 seconds. Sharp rising sweep. Given frequently in flight.
song_metrics.note_structure: RISING OOO-EEK: Sharp ascending squeal. Starts mid-frequency, sweeps steeply upward. Plaintive, almost electronic quality. Not a quack -- a squealing whistle.
song_metrics.note_types: Male flight call ooo-eek (diagnostic), female oo-eek (louder, more common), female brood call kuk-kuk-kuk, various alarm calls.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual calls in flight. Repeated when flushed or flying through wooded areas. Not complex phrasing.
song_metrics.timbre: Squealing. Thin. Ascending. Plaintive. Almost electronic. Unlike any other common duck vocalization. The rising quality is diagnostic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- moderate. Piercing and carrying despite moderate volume. The high frequency and rising pitch cut through forest canopy. Diagnostic quality over raw volume.
song_metrics.tempo: Individual calls in rapid succession when flushed. Moderate repetition rate in normal flight.
song_metrics.context: IN-FLIGHT CONTACT CALL. Given when flying through or over wooded areas. Female calls more frequently. Also alarm and brood contexts.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal documented variation. Flight calls species-typical.
song_metrics.key_property: RISING OOO-EEK FLIGHT CALL -- sharp ascending squeal, 1,500-5,000 Hz. E=4. Fourth consecutive Anatidae. Most beautiful duck in North America. Iridescent plumage. Cavity nester. Conservation success. Genus Aix. Wooded pond, summer.

plumage.overall: Male: iridescent green-to-purple head with swept-back crest, two thin white racing stripes (bill to nape), bright red eyes, chestnut-maroon breast with white speckles, warm buff flanks with vermiculated patterns, dark iridescent blue-green back. Female: gray-brown with distinctive white teardrop-shaped eye ring, blue speculum, subtle iridescent crown wash.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 121: (1) MALE: Iridescent green/purple head with white racing stripes (unmistakable). (2) MALE: Bright red eyes. (3) MALE: Chestnut breast. (4) FEMALE: White teardrop eye ring (diagnostic). (5) BOTH: Medium-sized duck on wooded ponds.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Males molt to eclipse plumage in late summer -- resemble females but retain red eyes and more prominent crest. Eclipse = temporary anonymity for the most beautiful duck.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct flight through wooded areas. Wings broad. Frequently gives the rising ooo-eek call in flight.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized perching duck. Body length 47-54 cm. Wingspan 66-73 cm. Weight 450-680 g (male), 430-580 g (female). Larger than Hooded Merganser (120), smaller than Common Merganser (119).
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 121: medium duck on wooded pond with iridescent green/purple head, white racing stripes, and red eyes (male) or white teardrop eye ring (female). If it's the most beautiful duck you've ever seen -- Wood Duck.
physical_description.field_id: Medium duck on wooded ponds. Male: iridescent head (green/purple), white racing stripes, red eyes, chestnut breast. Female: gray-brown, white teardrop eye ring. Rising ooo-eek flight call. Cavity nester in mature trees near water.

nodes.title: The Painted Swimmer -- Most Beautiful Duck in North America
nodes.description: Iridescent green-to-purple head with white racing stripes, bright red eyes, chestnut breast, buff flanks. Every surface carries color. Species name sponsa = 'betrothed.' Sexual selection has produced extraordinary plumage extravagance.

nodes.title: The Rising Ooo-Eek Flight Call
nodes.description: Sharp rising squeal, 1,500-5,000 Hz, 0.3-0.5 seconds. In-flight contact call. Plaintive, almost electronic. Not a quack -- a squealing whistle. Diagnostic. E=4.

nodes.title: Anatidae Streak -- Four Consecutive (118-121)
nodes.description: Harlequin Duck (118, turbulent stream), Common Merganser (119, river), Hooded Merganser (120, wooded pond), Wood Duck (121, wooded pond). Four genera: Histrionicus, Mergus, Lophodytes, Aix. Longest waterfowl streak. The pond holds for a second degree.

nodes.title: Cavity Nesting -- Nest Boxes and the Leap of Faith
nodes.description: Obligate cavity nester. Uses woodpecker cavities and nest boxes. Conservation success: nest box programs reversed population decline. Day-old ducklings leap 15+ meters from nest on mother's call. Dump nesting complicates management.

nodes.title: Genus Aix -- Two Beautiful Ducks, Two Continents
nodes.description: Wood Duck (North America) + Mandarin Duck (East Asia). Both extraordinarily beautiful. Both cavity nesters. Both perching ducks. Biogeographic mirror: parallel evolution on two continents through sexual selection.

nodes.title: Wooded Pond -- Second Consecutive
nodes.description: Wooded pond ecosystem for the second consecutive degree (120-121). Hooded Merganser and Wood Duck share habitat: sheltered water, mature trees, cavity nesting. The ecosystem has stabilized after the hydrological descent (116-120).

nodes.title: Conservation Success Story
nodes.description: Wood Duck recovered from severe depletion through Migratory Bird Treaty Act (1918) and massive nest box programs (1930s+). Founding narrative of North American waterfowl conservation. Habitat infrastructure reverses population decline.

cross_references.relationship: CAVITY NESTER DUO. Both cavity nesters on wooded ponds. Merganser: fan-crest, frog-call. Wood Duck: iridescent plumage, ooo-eek. Same ecosystem, same nesting strategy, different tribes. Consecutive degrees (120-121).
ANATIDAE STREAK. Four consecutive: Harlequin (118), Common Merganser (119), Hooded Merganser (120), Wood Duck (121). Four genera, four niches. The streak deepens.
ANATIDAE STREAK ORIGIN. Streak began at 118 with turbulent streams. At 121, calmed to wooded ponds. Four degrees, four genera, complete hydrological descent.

CAVITY CHAIN. Picidae nonet (107-115) built cavity infrastructure. Anatidae streak (118-121) nests in it. Fire creates snags, woodpeckers excavate, waterfowl occupy. Ecological succession across degrees.
FLICKER CAVITIES. Flickers create cavities used by Hooded Mergansers (smaller holes). Pileated Woodpeckers create larger cavities used by Wood Ducks. The Picidae-Anatidae cavity pipeline is explicit.
